18-40.summary: Focus on restoring B-vitamin status (especially thiamine), ensuring adequate protein and energy intake, and supporting liver health. Avoid further liver toxins.
do: Include whole grains, legumes, nuts, and seeds to increase thiamine (vitamin B1) intake.
Eat lean protein sources (pulses, eggs, poultry, fish) to support liver regeneration and muscle mass.
Emphasise fruits and vegetables rich in antioxidants (citrus fruits, berries, green leafy vegetables).
Drink adequate water throughout the day unless your doctor advises fluid restriction.
dont: Avoid alcohol completely, including 'small amounts' or 'social drinking'.
Limit highly processed foods high in sugar and saturated fat that worsen fatty liver.
Avoid herbal supplements claiming to 'detox the liver' without medical supervision.

sources.name: NIAAA – Helping Patients Who Drink Too Much (Clinician's Guide)
NIH MedlinePlus – Alcohol Use Disorder and Nutrition

41-65.summary: Older adults recovering from alcohol use disorder need careful attention to B-vitamins, bone health (calcium/vitamin D), and managing comorbidities like diabetes or hypertension.
do: Ensure adequate thiamine (B1) and other B-vitamins through fortified cereals, whole grains, and lean meats.
Include calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens) and vitamin D sources (fatty fish, fortified foods).
Maintain consistent meal timing to support blood sugar stability.
Stay hydrated, but monitor fluid intake if there are heart or kidney concerns.
dont: Avoid alcohol completely.
Don't skip meals, which can worsen blood sugar fluctuations.
Avoid excessive sodium if managing hypertension.

18-40.summary: Nicotine withdrawal can affect appetite and metabolism. Focus on balanced meals, managing cravings with healthy snacks, and supporting cardiovascular health.
do: Eat regular, balanced meals to stabilize blood sugar and reduce cravings.
Include fiber-rich foods (whole grains, fruits, vegetables) to support digestive health.
Stay hydrated with water; avoid excessive caffeine which can worsen anxiety.
Include antioxidant-rich foods (berries, leafy greens, nuts) to support recovery.
dont: Don't replace smoking with excessive caffeine or sugary snacks.
Avoid skipping meals, which can trigger cravings.
Limit highly processed foods that can contribute to inflammation.

sources.name: American Heart Association – Nutrition and Smoking Cessation

41-65.summary: Older adults quitting nicotine should focus on cardiovascular health, maintaining muscle mass, and managing metabolic changes.
do: Emphasize heart-healthy fats (olive oil, avocados, nuts) and lean proteins.
Include plenty of fruits and vegetables for antioxidants and fiber.
Maintain consistent meal timing to support metabolism.
Consider smaller, more frequent meals if appetite changes.
dont: Avoid excessive weight gain by choosing nutrient-dense snacks over empty calories.
Don't replace smoking with excessive alcohol or caffeine.

18-40.summary: Opioid recovery requires attention to digestive health (constipation is common), restoring normal appetite, and supporting overall nutritional status.
do: Include high-fiber foods (whole grains, legumes, fruits, vegetables) to address constipation.
Eat regular meals to restore normal eating patterns disrupted by opioid use.
Include probiotic-rich foods (yogurt, fermented foods) to support gut health.
Stay well-hydrated to support digestive function.
dont: Avoid excessive processed foods that can worsen digestive issues.
Don't skip meals, which can affect mood and energy levels.
Avoid excessive sugar, which can contribute to mood swings.

41-65.summary: Older adults in opioid recovery need careful attention to digestive health, bone health, and managing comorbidities.
do: Prioritize fiber-rich foods to manage constipation common with opioids.
Include calcium and vitamin D sources for bone health.
Eat regular, balanced meals to support stable blood sugar and energy.
Stay hydrated, but monitor fluid intake if there are kidney concerns.
dont: Avoid excessive processed foods.
Don't ignore digestive symptoms; consult a doctor if persistent.

18-40.summary: Cannabis recovery focuses on restoring normal appetite patterns, supporting mental health through nutrition, and maintaining energy levels.
do: Eat regular, balanced meals even if appetite is reduced initially.
Include omega-3 rich foods (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseeds) which may support mood.
Emphasize whole foods over processed snacks.
Stay hydrated and maintain consistent meal timing.
dont: Don't replace cannabis use with excessive caffeine or energy drinks.
Avoid skipping meals, which can affect mood and energy.
Limit highly processed foods that can contribute to inflammation.

41-65.summary: Older adults recovering from cannabis use should focus on maintaining healthy eating patterns and supporting overall wellness.
do: Maintain regular meal timing to support metabolism and energy.
Include nutrient-dense foods to meet nutritional needs.
Stay hydrated and include fiber-rich foods for digestive health.
dont: Avoid replacing cannabis with other substances or excessive caffeine.
Don't skip meals.
